title: Metallic powder in carrageenan marbling
sources: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894) — public domain. Turkish and Persian marbling traditions used gold leaf on size; mica powder is the modern equivalent.
notes: Adding mica or metallic powder to acrylic marbling pigment to create a metallic sheen in the veins of the marbled pattern. Covers: mixing mica powder into a small amount of already-extended acrylic pigment (the powder must be fully suspended, not dry clumps), adding extra ox-gall to the metallic mix (metallic pigments have higher surface tension), applying the metallic colour last (drops placed over base-colour drops), the resulting effect (gold veins through coloured background), cleaning the tray afterwards (metallic particles cling to the tray). Note: metallic colours do not comb as cleanly as standard pigments — best used as an accent on stone patterns or Spanish wave, not for precise combed patterns.
